one_line_takeaway: Proposes signed, lineage-carrying 'authenticated prompts' and hash-chained 'authenticated context' plus a provably monotonic policy-intersection algebra (4 theorems) to give agentic LLM systems cryptographic, not merely probabilistic, non-escalation guarantees against prompt injection and context poisoning -- but the paper's own 100%-detection/1.8%-overhead evidence rests on a small self-authored attack set and overhead numbers borrowed from an unpublished companion report, not an independent empirical evaluation.
key_finding: Across the 6 self-defined attack categories / 24 hand-authored variants, the authors report 100% detection with zero false positives and 'nominal overhead (1.8%)' -- the overhead figure is attributed to the companion MACAW paper [26] rather than independently measured here. The paper's substantive result is the formal one: policy intersection composed along a derivation chain provably yields monotonically non-increasing permissions and irreversible (transitive) denial propagation, giving a provable no-privilege-escalation-via-chaining guarantee that does not depend on catching any particular attack string or rephrasing -- e.g., in the paper's own worked example, search('auth') -> list('./config') -> read('cred.txt') is blocked at the final step because the root policy's denial of '*credential*' provably propagates through P1/P2/P3 regardless of how the LLM phrases the intermediate steps.
core_contribution: The paper (Rajagopalan & Rao, arXiv:2602.10481) introduces two cryptographic primitives for securing agentic LLM workflows at the abstraction boundary between LLMs and tools, rather than trying to make the LLM itself secure: (1) 'authenticated prompts' -- signed prompt objects P=(text, id, parent, policy, sigma) where sigma=Sign(H(text||id||parent||policy||metadata), K_agent), that embed parent and root lineage so derived prompts carry unforgeable proof of ancestry and inherited policy; and (2) 'authenticated context' -- agent memory bound to a principal, updated via a SHA-256 hash chain (H_{n+1}=SHA256(H_n||sigma_invocation||result)) with monotonically increasing sequence numbers and attestations, giving tamper-evident, replay-resistant state. On top of these primitives the paper formalizes a 'policy algebra' over policies P=(A,D,C) (allowed resources, denied resources, constraints) where child policy = parent-policy ∩ tool-policy ∩ org-policy, and proves four theorems: Monotonic Restriction (permissions never increase through derivation), Transitive Denial (a denial at any ancestor propagates to all descendants), Bounded Derivation (derivation depth is capped), and No Privilege Escalation (a root-level denial cannot be reached by any descendant/tool-chain). The system is implemented as an extension of the authors' own prior MACAW platform (self-cited as Rajagopalan et al. [26], an unpublished technical report) inside its Policy Enforcement Point (PEP) architecture, adding a 5-layer defense-in-depth stack (pattern matching -> distributed PEPs -> authenticated prompts -> authenticated context -> optional LLM-based semantic drift validation).
threat_model: Adversary (A1) has complete control over external data sources ingested by the agent (documents, API responses, databases) and can embed malicious instructions in that content; (A2) can shape multi-turn conversation history and poison resources shared across agents (vector DBs, knowledge bases); (A3) knows the available tools, interfaces and governing policies; (A4) can indirectly poison shared resources affecting multiple agents. The adversary is explicitly assumed unable to break the cryptographic primitives (signatures, hash functions), unable to compromise the trust anchor (the global agent registry / key distribution), and unable to compromise the LLM provider itself (model outputs are assumed unmodified). Defense goals stated: (O1) execution integrity, (O2) privilege non-escalation, (O3) intent preservation, (O4) context integrity, (O5) audit completeness. The authors explicitly scope this as Byzantine resistance 'at the protocol level' -- their primitives stop an adversarial agent from violating cryptographic invariants (forging lineage, escalating beyond granted policy, replaying stale state, corrupting another principal's context) but do not verify user intent or prevent misuse of legitimately granted permissions; they state policy authoring correctness/completeness is explicitly out of scope.
limitations: The paper itself concedes: (1) the biggest limitation is policy authoring -- the primitives enforce whatever policy is given perfectly, but cannot guarantee the policy is complete or correct ('garbage in, garbage out'); proving policy completeness is explicitly out of scope. (2) Lattice-theoretic proofs of the policy algebra (closure, associativity, commutativity, idempotence) are deferred to a promised camera-ready version, i.e. not actually included here. Beyond the authors' own admissions: (3) the '100%/zero-false-positive/1.8% overhead' evaluation is against a small (24-variant), self-authored attack set with no external dataset, no independent red team, and (for overhead) numbers borrowed from an unpublished companion report [26] rather than measured in this paper -- this is a much weaker evidentiary basis than the paper's framing ('achieves 100% detection... with nominal overhead') suggests. (4) The system depends on trust anchors the paper places out of scope for attack (a global agent registry for key lookup, uncompromised LLM providers), so its guarantees do not cover key-compromise, registry-compromise, or a malicious/compromised model provider. (5) Semantic Drift detection (Layer 5) explicitly relies on an LLM as the drift scorer, which the paper itself calls 'a measurement tool, not a security boundary' -- i.e., the one layer that handles paraphrase/semantic evasion is admittedly probabilistic, not covered by the cryptographic/formal guarantees claimed elsewhere. (6) The work builds directly on, and reuses the policy grammar and reported overhead of, the authors' own prior unpublished MACAW technical report [26], so the core enforcement substrate (PEPs, signed invocations) is inherited rather than newly evaluated here.

datasets_used: No external/public dataset used; evaluation is against a self-constructed set of 24 hand-authored attack variants across 6 categories (Injection x4, Obfuscation x5, Semantic Drift x5, Context Poisoning x3, Tool Chaining x4, Replay x3)
quantitative_results: 100% detection rate across the 6 attack categories / 24 variants (self-reported, self-authored test set)
1.8% overhead, explicitly stated to be carried over from the companion MACAW report [26] rather than measured in this paper
O(1) per-prompt space complexity for lineage tracking; total tracked state bounded at 3xN for a chain of N-sized prompt metadata regardless of derivation depth
baselines_compared: No quantitative baseline comparison is performed. The Related Work section discusses, but does not benchmark against: Rebuff, Lakera Guard, Vigil, LLM Guard, Attention Tracker (input filtering/classifier defenses); LangChain, LangGraph, Semantic Kernel, NeMo Guardrails, AutoGPT, BabyAGI (policy-based agent frameworks, incl. citing CVE-2024-8309 in LangChain's GraphCypherQAChain); Constitutional AI, RLHF, SecAlign, Google Gemini's indirect-injection classifiers, CAMEL (training/alignment-based defenses); C2PA v2.1 and blockchain audit logs (provenance/attestation systems).
